hook: Tehran Times and Asia Times — opposite political universes — both said the same thing about Strait of Hormuz / US-Iran deal. Nobody else did.

tweets.text: 32 buckets, 58 articles, 8 distinct frames in today's coverage.

Frame: DEAL_OR_NO_DEAL_STALEMATE (7 buckets). "US awaiting response from Iran over proposals for ceasefire deal, says Rubio

Diplomatic efforts continue despite fighting in and around co…" — The Guardian World

Frame: MILITARY_ESCALATION_SPIRAL (7 buckets). "Iran's IRGC Warns US Assets Against Strikes on Its Vessels in Persian Gulf, Says Missiles 'Locked' Onto Targets
The IRGC warned the US that…" — Republic World (alt)

Frame: ENERGY_PRICE_CONTAGION (6 buckets). "SAS cancels 1,200 flights in May due to fuel shortages

Scandinavia's SAS airline is cancelling nearly 1,200 flights in May as it braces fo…" — The Local Sweden

Frame: ALTERNATIVE_ROUTE_SCRAMBLE (2 buckets). "Gwadar Port gains strategic weight amid Hormuz crisis

GWADAR: Amid rising tensions in the Strait of Hormuz, the strategic importance of Gw…" — Dawn

The paradox: Tehran state media (celebrating Persian Gulf National Day with a sovereignty narrative) and a critical Asia-Pacific outlet (lambasting Trump's failed coercion attempts) reach the same conclusion from opposite directions: US pressure has not forced Iranian capitulati…

Silence as data: `china` didn't cover this — they covered CGTN covers Wang Yi's statement that Hormuz blockade is not in common interests and UKMTO port restrictions—institutional diplomatic framing that avoids any editorial stance on the conflict. instead.

**Asia Times** (asia_pacific_regional): Introduces the petroyuan war-reparations angle: Iran and China coordinating yuan-denominated payments as financial retaliation for US sanctions—a frame absent from all other buckets.
